coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) pillared Ta4C3Tx MXene as high-performance electrochemical supercapacitors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Fatima, Sabeen
Waqas Hakim, M.
Zheng, Xiaoxiao
Sun, Yu
Li, Ziheng
Han, Nan
Li, Muyang
Wang, Zeyuan
Han, Lei
Wang, Liang
Khan, Safia
Liu, Jiangwei
Li, Hu
coredata.dc:description: Two-dimensional (2D) materials are proved to possess outstanding energy storage capabilities for advanced supercapacitors, yet these materials require modification to overcome field obstacles and reach their full potential. Here, we prepared tantalum carbide MXene and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) by adopting facile synthesis routes. Surface engineering of 2D tantalum carbide sheets is carefully performed with zero-dimensional graphene to achieve NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets. The compositional analysis demonstrated the attachment of quantum dots with MXene sheets. The modified 1.5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 3%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 7%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx and 10%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets presented enhanced energy storage capability than NGQD and plain MXene. Graphene quantum dots enable Ta4C3Tx to have faster more stable reaction kinetics and enhance the gravimetric capacitance 7 folds (701 F/g) compared to plain MXene (100 F/g). Due to the exceptional working electrode performances, an asymmetric supercapacitor is also fabricated with NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets as working electrode. The device exhibited a pseudocapacitive battery-like storage mechanism with a specific capacity of 110 Cg-1, power and energy densities of 9000 W/kg and 55 W-h/Kg respectively. Due to the low corrosion rate exhibited by Tafel plots an excellent capacity retention is observed up to 20000 cycles. The study imparts valuable insights into engineering 0D/2D nanostructures for next-generation energy storage systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped graphene Quantum Dots (NGQD)
Tantalum carbide (Ta4C3Tx) MXene
Supercapacitors
Electrochemical energy storage
MXene electrodes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grant No.: ZR2021QE148 and ZR2022ZD05 ), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation for Excellent Young Scientists Fund Program (Overseas) (Grant No.: 2022HWYQ-060 ), Shandong Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No.: SDCX-ZG-202400293 ), Guangdong International Science and Technology Cooperation Program (Grant No.: 2023A0505050087 ), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (Grant No.: 2022A1515011473 ), and Shenzhen Fundamental Research Program (Grant No.: JCYJ20230807094119040 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) NGQD, tantalum carbide (Ta4C3Tx) MXene, and NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets. (b) UV–vis and PL analysis (inset) of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD).
SEM images of (a) NGQD thin film on Si wafer, (b) etched tantalum carbide (Ta4C3Tx) MXene, (c) exfoliated Ta4C3Tx MXene, and (d) NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets.

HR-TEM images of (a) tantalum carbide (Ta4C3Tx) MXene, (b–d) NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets showing MXene sheets and NGQD distribution over nanosheets, (e) SAED pattern of NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, (f–g) elemental mapping of Ta4C3Tx and NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets, and (h–i) TEM elemental micrographs of Ta4C3Tx and NGQD/Ta4C3Tx.

XPS comparison of Ta4C3Tx MXene and NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets for (a) Ta4f, (b) C1s, (c) O1s, deconvolution of the O1s spectral peaks of (d) Ta4C3Tx, and (e) NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets, and (f) N1s spectra of NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets.
(a) FTIR plot of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots NGQD, Ta4C3Tx MXene, and NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets. Raman spectra of (b) Ta4C3Tx MXene, and (c) NGQD/Ta4C3Tx.

Cyclic voltammetry curves of (a) nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots NGQD, (b) Ta4C3Tx, (c) 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets. (d) CV comparison at 10 mVs−1, (e) Specific capacitance, and (f) specific capacity comparison of all the working electrodes.

Galvanostatic charge-discharge plots for (a) Ta4C3Tx MXene, and (b) 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets 1 M KOH. (c) GCD comparison of all the working electrodes at a current density of 3 A/g. (d) EIS Nyquist plot for all the electrode materials, and (e) high-frequency region after EIS magnification. (e) Cyclic performance of Ta4C3Tx and 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets in 1 KOH electrolyte.

Cyclic voltammetry curves of 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx//AC capacitor device (a) in different voltage windows from 0 V to 1.2 V, (b) at various scan rates. (c) charging and discharging curves at different current densities. (d) Calculated specific capacity, power density, and energy density. (e) EIS plots before and after the cyclic stability measurement of the device. (f) Cyclic stability test up to 20k cycles, Inset shows the schematic representation of the supercapacitor device fabrication.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) pillared Ta4C3Tx MXene as high-performance electrochemical supercapacitors

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Research Institute of Shandong University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two-dimensional (2D) materials are proved to possess outstanding energy storage capabilities for advanced supercapacitors, yet these materials require modification to overcome field obstacles and reach their full potential. Here, we prepared tantalum carbide MXene and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) by adopting facile synthesis routes. Surface engineering of 2D tantalum carbide sheets is carefully performed with zero-dimensional graphene to achieve NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets. The compositional analysis demonstrated the attachment of quantum dots with MXene sheets. The modified 1.5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 3%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 7%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx and 10%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets presented enhanced energy storage capability than NGQD and plain MXene. Graphene quantum dots enable Ta4C3Tx to have faster more stable reaction kinetics and enhance the gravimetric capacitance 7 folds (701 F/g) compared to plain MXene (100 F/g). Due to the exceptional working electrode performances, an asymmetric supercapacitor is also fabricated with NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets as working electrode. The device exhibited a pseudocapacitive battery-like storage mechanism with a specific capacity of 110 Cg-1, power and energy densities of 9000 W/kg and 55 W-h/Kg respectively. Due to the low corrosion rate exhibited by Tafel plots an excellent capacity retention is observed up to 20000 cycles. The study imparts valuable insights into engineering 0D/2D nanostructures for next-generation energy storage systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lately with the increase of population and excessive use of non-renewable energy sources such as fossil fuels created a need to develop sustainable and renewable energy systems [1]. These alternate resources include various solar, tidal and wind ways to create high performance and efficient energy technologies [2]. But with the evolution of the modern world and to meet the recent energy demands it is also crucial to work on energy storage technologies as well. A lot of energy storage systems has been produced till the date named as supercapacitors, hydrogen fuel storage systems, batteries and solar cells etc. [3] Due to difficulty in handling the hydrogen fuel as well as the limited efficiency of solar cells batteries and supercapacitors have attracted the most attention of researchers among electrochemical energy storage devices [4]. Further batteries due to their rechargeability, are a feasible option, but their restricted power density still needs to be resolved [5].

Supercapacitors are drawing in all the scientific community from all over the world due to their economical nature, high charging/discharging, enhanced power density and long-term consistency [6,7]. Supercapacitor performance depends on the electrode materials used in the devices. Based on these electrode materials, supercapacitor electrochemical behaviors are divided into pseudocapacitive, electric double layer capacitor (EDLC), and battery-like storage mechanisms [8]. Pseudocapacitors are comprised of one or a combination of surface redox or intercalation-type electrode materials, while EDLC stores charge through electrostatic double layer formation. On the other hand, battery-like materials predominantly store charge through faradaic redox reactions. To date, various materials such as polymers, metal oxide [9,10] carbon-based materials like graphene [11], carbon nanotubes (CNT) [12], metal carbides [13], and carbonitrides [14] have been explored to enhance the operational characteristics of the supercapacitors. Among the carbon-based materials 2D (two-dimensional) materials have been in the spotlight for a while due to their fascinating properties at the nanometer scale. These 2D materials belong to a class of layered sheet-like materials with enhanced surface area [15], excellent conductivity and physical strength [16] as well as outstanding electronic features [17]. Graphene and MXene falls to the 2D materials family and have been investigated in approximately all scientific applications, including optics, electronics [18], data storage [19], catalysis [20,21], and energy storage [22].

MXenes are basically transition metal carbide/nitride which originated from the parent MAX phase in which M is metal, A is aluminum or silicon, and X is carbide or nitride [23]. Aluminum is etched using various methods from the MAX phased to achieve the etched MXene form which can then be subjected to polymer exfoliation to separate the MXene sheets from one another to reach the final 2D MXene sheet structure [24]. Graphene was discovered before the MXene and is comprised of a hexagonal sheet of carbon atoms containing sp2 hybridization and can be synthesized into various modified forms including graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), graphene nanoplatelets (GNP) and graphene quantum dots (GQD) [25–27]. Improper synthesis, oxidation as well as restacking of sheets effect on the physical and electrical properties of these 2D materials. Hence, to fully utilize their potential and to overcome the synthesis problems, these 2D materials are usually combined with other materials to synthesize nanohybrids, nanocomposites, or nano-heterostructures. MXene such as titanium carbide (Ti3C2Tx/V2CTx) and its modified hybrid phases has been proven outstanding supercapacitor electrode materials in the recently published studies [28–30]. Tantalum carbide distinct mechanical, sensing, and thermal properties have made it a key focus of interest for many scientific disciplines. It has shown exceptional activities in the field of electrocatalysis owing to the large surface area of it sheet structure [31–33]. Ta-MXene is specifically considered to provide enhanced performances for having resistance against oxidative environments due to the stronger Ta-C bonds and inherent stability of Ta atom being a transition atom [34,35]. Under temperature treatment Ta4C3Tx MXene is proven to provide enhanced stability as well as barrier protection [34]. It has also exhibited enhanced capacitive performance as biocompatible supercapacitors [35]. Tantalum carbide MXenes also have great potential in medical science owing to its excellent functioning for photothermal tumor ablation [36]. Pristine MXenes have limitations due to the restacking of sheets which tends to reduce their conductivity and electrochemical performances. Graphene together with Ta4C3Tx tends to provide stable performances even under high temperatures [37]. Further, it is also reported that the graphene with tantalum can provide good stability against corrosion/oxidation [38]. Several studies on graphene quantum dots (GQD) also showed that their small dimensionality entails the band-gap modification and active sites for boosting the overall capacitance and cyclability in the different electrode materials [39–41]. The heteroatom doping (such as nitrogen) in 2D/0D materials changes conductivity and electron density in the material by providing more active sites with nitrogen functional groups [42]. Hence nitrogen doping in GQD regulates the redox active sites for pseudo-active charge storage as well as helps in enhancing the electrochemical stability [40]. In addition, NGQD incorporation avoids restacking of MXene sheets to achieve structural integrity and uniformity. Therefore, the synergy between NGQD and MXene can significantly enhance the electrode material functionality in supercapacitors to further overcome the modern challenges faced by the energy storage industry.

In this study we have prepared tantalum carbide (Ta4C3Tx) MXene via simple and conventional synthesis routes. In parallel nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) were also synthesized having a dimensionality < 5 nm. Controlled surface engineering of MXene sheets by using graphene quantum dots enabled us to create NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets with different concentrations of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots. The concentrations of NGQD were varied from 1.5% to 10% in MXene sheets. The crystal structure, morphology, elemental composition, and atomic vibrational modes were also studied by performing supporting characterizations of all prepared NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets. In addition, these 1.5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 3%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 7%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx , and 10%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets were used as electrode materials in alkaline media to evaluate their performances for supercapacitors. Overall, the NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets efficiency was synergistically enhanced as electrode material in alkaline media. Among all 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx outperforms others by showing excellent stability and enhanced capacitance, energy density and power density.

MAX phase was etched and exfoliated using hydrofluoric acid (HF 48 wt% in H2O, ≥99.99%) and 25% solution of tetramethylammonium hydroxide 25% aqueous solution (TMAOH) polymer as illustrated in Supplementary Information Fig. S1a. One gram (1g) MAX was slowly dispersed in HF (10 ml) under continuous stirring in a Teflon beaker and was etched at 45 °C for 50 h on the magnetic stirring plate. After that, excessive HF was removed via centrifugation at low rpm (3000–4500) and multiple washes were performed via deionized (DI) water at high rpm (10000–12000) to obtain MXene ink. Then MXene ink was mixed with polymer base TMAOH (6 ml) under sonication for 2 h and then transferred to stirring at room temperature up to 12 h to exfoliate the MXene sheets. The polymer was extracted using centrifugation and the exfoliated MXene sheets were further washed with DI water and vacuum filtered. The end product was dried in a vacuum oven for 8 h at around 60 °C to obtain powder form of 2D MXene sheets.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD)
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots, both citric acid (C6H8O7) as well as urea (CO(NH2)2) were mixed in a 1:1 ratio into DI water and after half hour stirring the mixture was transferred to an autoclave for hydrothermal synthesis. The hydrothermal process was carried out for 6.5 h at 180 °C and a light-yellow nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots solution was obtained as end product as shown in Fig. S1b.

A solvothermal approach was used to decorate the MXene nanosheets with nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots. NGQD were used in a controlled proportion of 1.5%, 3%, 5%, 7% and 10%, respectively. Also, Ta4C3Tx MXene solution was prepared by dispersing 50 mg MXene in 25 ml DI water under sonication. Then the NGQD solution was added dropwise into the MXene dispersion. The mixture was shifted to stirring and the temperature was slowly increased up to 80 °C and maintained for 2 h. After cooling down the sediments were washed with DI water and dried at 60 °C for 8 h. The final synthesized NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets were named as 1.5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 3%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 7%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx , and 10%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx according to NGQD proportions.

To analyze the crystal structure and nanosheets morphology X-ray diffraction (XRD) of Rigaku SmartLab and Nova NanoSEM 450 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) were used. Talos F200X transmission electron microscopy (TEM) instrument was used to obtain elemental information as well as to analyze smaller morphology details. Also, ultraviolet (UV–vis) spectroscopy as well as photoluminescence (PL) were observed for NQGD fluorescent properties. Thermo Scientific K-alpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) tool was used to check chemical bonding. To evaluate all the in plane/out-of-plane vibrational modes Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw inVia inspect Raman/PL microscope) as well as Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) were also performed for as-synthesized NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets.

To evaluate the electrochemical performances, a CH instruments multichannel potentiostat was used. A three-electrode system was employed, with a platinum plate serving as the counter electrode, Hg/HgO serving as the reference electrode, and nickel foam coating NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets slurry serving as the working electrode. For testing, an alkaline electrolyte was made with 1 M (M) potassium hydroxide (KOH). To prepare the working electrode slurry, 500 μL of ethanol, nafion and DI water (1:1:0.2) were sonicated for 20 min with 5 mg of powdered NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets. A 1 × 1 cm area of nickel foam was uniformly coated with working electrode slurry (100 μL) and then dried for half an hour at a moderate temperature. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were obtained at different scan rates (2 mV–200 mV) to determine capacitance. The voltage window selection for CV curves was done by analyzing the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) plot (Fig. S2). A suitable voltage range was observed via LSV to perform multiple CV curves over a broader voltage range and then it was narrowed down to obtain an optimized voltage window (0V–0.55V). The voltage range was selected to obtain visible symmetric oxidation and reduction peaks by avoiding side/irreversible reactions and electrode degradation. LSV measurements clearly show that the selected voltage window for CV measurements is a stable region for the capacitive reactions. Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) data was recorded at various current densities within the voltage window, similar to CV to further compute the capacity and energy density. Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) information was gathered employing the sin-wave in the frequency range of 1 × 105 Hz to 0.1Hz.

A two-electrode asymmetrical supercapacitor device was fabricated by using NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets as the working electrode and the activated carbon (AC) as the counter/reference electrode assembly. A typical filter paper was used as a separator between the device electrodes and 1M KOH was used as electrolyte. All the CV and GCD curves were conducted for the 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx//AC device within a selected working window. The EIS and cyclic stability tests were also performed, along with the calculations of specific capacity, energy density, and power density at different current densities.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the prepared material samples are presented in Fig. 1 a. NGQD crystal structure provided a big hump of (002) place around the 20°–50° showing a uniform size distribution of the graphene quantum dots with a d-spacing of 0.39 Å [43]. This (002) plane represents the C=C sp2 network of the graphene present in quantum dots. In MXene main (002) plane appears around 5° with other corresponding peaks [35,44]. The d-spacing of Ta4C3Tx MXene is around 17 Å with a crystallite size around 75 nm. The peaks width is increased while the intensity is a bit reduced in NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets due to disorderedness produced in the crystal structure with NGQD distribution. With the aluminum removal and exfoliation MAX planes are replaced with new MXene planes like (008) and (0010). The presence of NGQD (002) around 25° plane supports the successful synthesis of NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets. The ultraviolet visible spectroscopy and photoluminescence spectra of NGQD are shown in Fig. 1b. UV peak around 331.4 nm belongs to the n-π∗ (C=O) and π-π∗ (sp2 C=C) transitions of graphene in quantum dots [25,45]. While the PL excitation of NGQD thin film at 325 nm exhibited a sharp luminescence peak at around 449.5 nm corresponding to small dimensionality and the blue luminescence property of the nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots [27].

SEM image of a thin film of NGQD on silicon (Si) wafer is shown in Fig. 2 a. NGQD are distributed over the Si wafer in the form of clusters formed during the drying process of quantum dots while making the thin film. The various sizes of NGQD nano clusters are also measured. Fig. 2b reveals a side view of etched Ta4C3Tx MXene flake displaying several MXene sheets stacked in order with each other. Also, a little opening in between sheets can be seen, created during the aluminum etching process of MAX phase. The opening between the sheets is enhanced by the exfoliation process of Ta4C3Tx MXene as displayed in Fig. 2c. MXene sheets are exfoliated, crimpled and more disordered with the attachment of NGQD as visible in the SEM image of NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets (Fig. 2d) [18]. The morphology analysis is consistent with the XRD results.

To obtain deeper morphological details high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) for Ta4C3Tx and NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets was also performed and results are given in Fig. 3 . Transparent MXene layers at a scale of 100 nm in Fig. 3a–b depict sheet like morphology for Ta4C3Tx and NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets while in Fig. 3c atomic arrangements in MXene sheets are also visible. In NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets at a scale of 5 nm (Fig. 3d) two contrasts of atomic arrangements can be seen corresponding to the circular NGQD distribution over the Ta4C3Tx nanosheets [43]. The TEM image also supported that the NGQD sizes are smaller than the 5 nm. Fig. 3e shows a selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image referring to the crystal orientation pattern of NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets. In addition, angular dark field mapping results were also analyzed to evaluate the elemental composition of the MXene and NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets. The presence of tantalum (Ta), carbon (C) given in Fig. 3f–g explains the intact chemistry of MXene sheets along with the presence of oxygen (O), Al, and fluorine (F) terminations during the etching, exfoliation, and washing processes [46]. Moreover, the TEM elemental micrographs in Fig. 3h–i displayed sharp peaks of Ta (M, N, Lα and Lβ), C, and O with the traces of Al and F. The concentration peak of C and O is enhanced in NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets elemental display due to the presence of NGQD over the MXene sheets.

In order to explore the chemical composition, X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained and are given in Fig. 4 . In Ta4f spectral peaks (Fig. 4a) Ta-C is present in both Ta4C3Tx and NGQD/Ta4C3Tx with Ta-O referring to the intact chemical bonding of tantalum carbide layers of MXene [46]. In C1s there is a positive shift in sp2 C peak of NGQD/Ta4C3Tx in comparison to MXene and C-Tx peak intensity is a little increased which perhaps due to the bonding of NGQD carbon and nitrogen with the Ta4C3Tx sheets, as given in Fig. 4b. Fig. 4c–e exhibits O1s comparison of the Ta4C3Tx and NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets with the deconvolution of both spectra. A visible change can be seen in the oxygen bonding of both structures. Due to the nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots attachment with MXene the intensity of O-C-Ta is increased [47]. Also, the OH molecules' absorption also diminishes during the NGQD/Ta4C3Tx synthesis process. The N1s XPS deconvolution of NGQD/Ta4C3Tx is shown in Fig. 4f, referring to the presence of pyrrolic, pyridinic, and quaternary nitrogen peaks of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots in the NGQD/Ta4C3Tx chemical structure [48,49].

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Raman spectroscopy were also performed to explore the stretching in the structure as well as the vibrational modes of the material. In FTIR analysis (Fig. 5 a), all the C=C. C-N, C-OH and C-H stretching appeared in between 900 cm−1 to 1700 cm−1 with an big hump of OH stretching at around 3300 cm−1 [50]. In Ta4C3Tx the presence of tantalum-carbon (Ta-C) and carbon-oxygen (C-O) refers to the conventional stretching of MXene atomic/molecular structure. In addition, with these conventional stretching there is a visible difference of carbon stretching in C-Tx region of NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets corresponding to the presence of both atomic structures together. In Fig. 5b Raman spectra of Ta4C3Tx is presented corresponding to the E1g, E2g and A1g peaks with all the vibrational modes (ω5, ω10), (ω3, ω6, 0ω9), and (ω4, ω7, ω8) [51,52]. These vibrational modes are attached with the Ta-C, Ta-Ta, Ta-Al as well as C-O bonding of the MAX/MXene structure. With the aluminum etching and exfoliation of MXene sheets, these vibrational modes vary. In Raman of NGQD/Ta4C3Tx (Fig. 5c) all the vibrational modes are present in the flake and termination region, with the appearance of D and G bands in the carbon region due to the attachment of graphene quantum dots with the MXene sheets [48].

For electrochemical testing, cyclic voltammetry (CV) performances were obtained using a three-electrode system in 1 M potassium hydroxide KOH electrolyte. Several electrodes were prepared using NGQD, Ta4C3Tx, 1.5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 3%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 7%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx and 10%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx as working materials. CV curves were obtained at 2 mV/s, 5 mV/s, 10 mV/s, 20 mV/s, 50 mV/s, 100 mV/s, and 200 mV/s. The CV plots for nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) and Ta4C3Tx MXene are shown in Fig. 6 a–b. The working window was maintained from 0 V to 0.55 V. The oxidation and reduction peaks were recorded within this voltage range for all the active materials. The specific capacitances were calculated using the mass of active material, scan rate, area and the voltage of working window. The capacitive performances were enhanced for NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets in comparison to bare NGQD and MXene. The highest electrochemical performance was obtained for 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets as demonstrated in Fig. 6c while the CV plots for 1.5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 3%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 7%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, and 10%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx are presented in the Supplementary Information Fig. S3. To show the overall comparison of all the working materials a performance comparison is given in Fig. 6d at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. The attachment of NGQD with the Ta4C3Tx sheets provided more active sites for fast ionic diffusion as well as enhanced the reaction kinetics for more charge storage [40,53]. Among all working electrodes, 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx exhibited the best charge storage performance due to good conductivity, increased effective surface area, more exposure to active sites, reduced charge transfer resistance, and fast redox reactions. For lower combinations such as enhanced 1%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx and 3%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx electrical conductivity and charge transfer may not be sufficient due to fewer active sites present while for high concentrations 7%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx and 10%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx due to excessive loading the active sites may have blocked resulting in high resistance and reduced conductivity. Further, gravimetric capacitances were also given in Fig. 6e for all the working electrodes at various scan rates. The specific capacitances of as-synthesized NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanostructures were comparatively higher than the bare Ta4C3Tx attributed to the high storage capability of the NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets. The bar plot in Fig. 6f depicted that at a scan rate of 2 mV/s the specific capacity is 81 C/g for Ta4C3Tx, 123 C/g for 1.5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 183 C/g for 3%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 345 C/g for 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 246 C/g for 7%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx and 156 C/g for 10%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx.

To further explore the charge storage capabilities of working electrodes, galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements were taken at different current densities. GCD plots for Ta4C3Tx and 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx are shown in Fig. 7 a–b while for GCD curves for 1.5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 3%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 7%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, and 10%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx are given in Supplementary Information Fig. S4. For a comparison, among all the working electrodes GCD curves at a current density 3 A/g are plotted in Fig. 7c to explore the performance difference between all these electrodes. At a same current density 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets demonstrated the best charging-discharging behavior attributed to its highest charge storage capability for supercapacitors. EIS curves of all the working electrodes are shown in Fig. 7d. Overall, the impedances are very much decreased in NGQD/Ta4C3Tx in comparison to NGQD and Ta4C3Tx. The lowest impedance was observed for 5 %-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx working electrode in support to its best energy storage performance in alkaline media. Furthermore, Fig. 7e provides a zoomed-in perspective of Nyquist plots at higher frequencies, which depict the semicircles caused by charge transfer at the electrode-electrolyte interface [54]. The active materials' pseudocapacitive behavior is shown by the formation of semicircles, the narrower the semicircle, the lower the charge transfer resistance [55]. Therefore, 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx showed the lowest charge transfer resistance among all the NGQD/Ta4C3Tx working electrodes. The semi-circle is incomplete in 10%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx which may be due to the ion diffusion or more higher frequencies are required for its completion. In addition, the capacity retention test was also performed to check the stability and durability of the working electrodes. The columbic efficiency for Ta4C3Tx MXene and 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets were recorded up to 20,000 cycles as shown in Fig. 7f. 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx displayed excellent capacity retention (86% after 20,000 cycles) capability in comparison to bare Ta4C3Tx (61% after 20,000 cycles) attributed towards stable and fast reaction kinetics. The surface passivation in Ta-based MXene along with the self-healing properties can enhance the long-term stability for NGQD/Ta4C3Tx. In addition, GQDs and MXene strong interface bonding could help significantly reduce the surface oxidation. Also, the GQDs could act as electron reservoirs to provide more stable reactions at the electrode surface. A submersion test was conducted by submerging the 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx electrode in the 1M KOH for two weeks and the Tafel and EIS plots (given in Fig. S5) were measured to assess the corrosion current and resistance of the electrode. The high polarization resistance (8.82 × 106 Ω cm2) with low corrosion rate (6.3 x 10−5 mmpy) further supports the slow degradation of the electrode, validating the long-term capacity retention.

Among all the working electrodes, 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets exhibited the best charge storage capability, so an asymmetric supercapacitor device was fabricated to further evaluate its performance as a working electrode in a practical device assembly. In Fig. 8 a the 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx//AC device performance over different operating voltage windows (from 1.2 V to 0.55 V) at a scan rate of 100 mVs−1 is shown. The device exhibited a non-rectangular CV behavior (Fig. 8b) which is further analyzed over various scan rates (5 mVs−1 to 150 mVs−1) at an operating voltage window from 0 V to 1 V. The appearance of the visible redox peaks in CV curves even at higher scan rates, demonstrated the faradaic redox reactions at both electrodes and the pseudocapacitive battery-like nature of the capacitor device [8]. Also, the GCD curves at various current densities in Fig. 8c validate the pseudocapacitive characteristics of 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx//AC device. Plus, specific capacity, power density as well as charge density calculations were also done and are displayed in Fig. 8d. The specific capacity at a current density range from 1 A/g to 5 A/g varies from 45 C/g to 110 C/g. The calculated power density ranges from 1800 W/kg to 9000 W/kg while the energy density changes from 22.5 W-h/Kg to 55 W-h/Kg. EIS plot in Fig. 8e displayed the impedance of the device before (green color) and after (maroon color) the cyclic stability measurements at a frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 10000 Hz. A schematic assembly of the asymmetric capacitor device is also presented in Fig. 8f inset along with the capacity retention plot. The device maintained 82% cyclic stability even after 20000 repeated cycles. The impedance measurement validates that the impedance of the device has been slightly increased after the capacity retention measurements, while the capacitive nature of the device is still well maintained. Hence, enhanced capacity, high power and energy density, and long-term cycling stability of the 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx make it a promising candidate for next-generation energy storage systems.

In this study, we have prepared Ta4C3Tx MXene and nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQD) using easy and cost-effective synthesis routes. Ta4C3Tx MXene sheets were surface engineered via NGQD by adopting a solvothermal synthesis approach. Various NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets were created by carefully regulating the surface engineering of Ta4C3Tx sheets with NGQD varying concentrations from 1.5% to 10%. For the assessment of working materials quality, shape, crystallinity, chemical structure, and elemental content, different supporting characterizations such as UV–vis, PL, Raman, XRD, TEM, FTIR, SEM and XPS were also performed. The effective synthesis and excellent quality of every material were explained by these characterizations. To test the charge storage capabilities NGQD, Ta4C3Tx, 1.5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 3%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, 7%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx, and 10%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx nanosheets were employed as working electrodes in 1 M KOH electrolyte. With the attachment of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots the charge storage of Ta4C3Tx nanosheets was not only enhanced up to sevenfold but also displayed excellent stability for 20k repeated cycles. The performance evaluation of 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx//AC capacitor device demonstrated charge storage characteristics in between pseudocapacitance and battery-like properties, along with long-term stability. Also, the increased specific capacity, power, and energy density of the device make 5%-NGQD/Ta4C3Tx a superior candidate for energy storage device applications. All things considered, this work offers guidance on how to modify dense MXene phases, such as tantalum carbide, to improve their electrochemical device performances relative to their plain forms.

Sabeen Fatima: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. M. Waqas Hakim: Validation, Formal analysis. Xiaoxiao Zheng: Writing – review & editing. Yu Sun: Writing – review & editing. Ziheng Li: Software. Nan Han: Visualization. Muyang Li: Software. Zeyuan Wang: Writing – review & editing. Lei Han: Software. Liang Wang: Visualization. Safia Khan: Writing – review & editing. Jiangwei Liu: Resources, Formal analysis. Hu Li: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
